next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 12

Yusuf had pushed the conference table against the far wall and arranged chairs in a rough semicircle. It hadn't helped much. Eight people in a room built for six, and nobody looked comfortable.

Kai sat between Lena and Yuki Tanaka, a Japanese sprinter whose left hand still went numb without warning, a gift from the virus. Meridian's patch hadn't fixed it because Yuki hadn't signed. On Lena's other side sat two middle-distance runners Kai knew by event but not by name, a woman with close-cropped red hair who ran the 1500 and a South African in his late twenties who ran the 800. Behind them all, in a chair she'd pulled a full meter back from the group, Margot sat with her legs crossed and her arms folded, as though she'd come to audit rather than participate.

Jin-seo Park had the chair nearest the door. Lena had invited her, which had surprised everyone, including Jin-seo, who sat holding a paper cup of coffee she'd stopped drinking twenty minutes ago.

Through the west-facing windows, the Bernese Alps stood in late-afternoon light the color of old tin. The lower ridges were patchy where snowmelt had started, grey rock showing through the white in irregular blotches. Water ticked off the eaves in an uneven rhythm that Kai had been trying not to listen to since he sat down.

Yusuf looked tired. He'd been working nights in the calibration bay. It showed in the red threading his eyes, the shirt untucked on one side, the way his Birmingham accent sat heavier than usual. He had his laptop open on a stool.

"Right," he said. "So. Short version."

He turned the lapto...
open_hooks_to_carry: Kai's synthetic muscle moves his hands before conscious intent during sex—grip adjusting with precision that is not his
Lena's desync surfaces during intimacy: she goes still, eyes change, the person behind them briefly absent
Lena describes the desync from inside: being gently moved aside while something smoother takes her place
Kai describes the blackout void: the terror was that his body performed well without him
prev_tail_excerpt:
Lena looked at the port mirrored dark in the window. Her body on the bed. Her body on the screen. Her body in the lane that morning doing work she had not lived through. Every version felt hers until the system disagreed. Then the recorded one outranked the lived one.

She moved the cursor back to the box and added a second sentence.

Coach was present on feed.

She stopped there.

If she submitted that line, Petra would pull the raw session if raw session still existed. If it did not, then the official record would stay what Margot had made it and Lena would have filed herself into a hole with no ladder. Either way the form in front of her was no longer just medicine. It was an accusation she could not yet prove.

Margot's pressure sat in the room with her now, no longer abstract. Immediate hold. Clean summary. Contradiction becoming the whole story.

Her thumb found the edge of the port at the base of her skull.

The display pulsed green.

Nominal.

She closed the form without submitting it.

The system asked if she wanted to save as draft.

No.

The form disappeared. The green status bar remained. For a while she looked at the word nominal and did nothing else.

Then she left the session unflagged.

Her thumb stayed pressed to the port until the skin went sore. The display did not change.
state_deltas_required: Enhancement technology intrudes on the most intimate human experience
Body autonomy becomes intimate and terrifying; connection deepens through shared horror rather than despite it
